reviews.review_text:
Veilguard is less like a sequel and more like a sanitized parody made by people who no longer understand why Dragon Age mattered in the first place. Dragon Age world used to be cruel, political, oppressive, dangerous, companions distrusted each other. The world did not care about your comfort. Veilguard strips away that identity and replaces it with overexplained dialogue, emotionally curated companions, and scenes that constantly soften their own tension before anything meaningful can settle. Characters repeat the obvious, explain every feeling out loud, and move past deaths or catastrophes with barely any emotional aftermath. Veilguard shrinks a brutal dark fantasy world into a therapy circle constantly reaffirming their teammates filled with teenage-like drama dialogues.

Even the visual direction feels wrong. Many characters look overly stylized, polished, and exaggerated in a way that feels ripped from modern Marvel or Disney-style ensemble media instead of Dragon Age. Previous games looked grounded, worn down, and hostile. Veilguard devs imitated character design they thought liked by "modern audiences".

And the leadership fantasy has completely lost its weight. Instead of commanding unstable factions during a collapsing world, much of the game feels like sitting in a lounge listening to personal problems while the apocalypse patiently waits for everyone to finish emotionally checking in. Rook is handed authority with almost no resistance, and factions easily fall into place.

What makes this genuinely painful as a longtime fan is that you can still see pieces of Dragon Age buried underneath all of it. The lore is there. The Inquisition is there. Solas is there. The foundations are still there. But the soul is gone. Worse, Veilguard reveals major pieces of long-awaited lore yet fails to give them the weight, atmosphere, or execution they deserve.

Once, Alistair described its world “pretty as a painting.” It was truly heartfelt back then. Now, Dragon Age has eroded into a parody-like just to chase “modern audiences”. As a result, it lost its identity, the legacy that made the series special in the first place.

Dragon Age Inquisition was the last real Dragon Age game. If you value what the series used to be, Veilguard is not it.

I honestly liked every dragon age game up to this one, and im generally pretty open to liking things that most don't like but man is this thing a hot steaming pile of rubbish. i played it for a couple hours before feeling disappointed and intended to come back to it the next day and the idea of playing this game more made me feel extremely sad and exhausted. I'm not "anti woke" or whatever but this game is everything wrong with modern gaming. The combat is fine its kinda enjoyable but abit mindless but the story is just so horrifically bad, none of hte characters are slightly likeable and the way they talk is so bizarre for the setting, even if you are a diehard dragon age fan you should avoid at all costs

If one is to try to be kind to this game, one could say that it was ahead of its time in that it perhaps could have been written by AI even before AI was a thing. It is not a bad game at all, but it is not a good game either. It is a bland game. 

When remembering previous games in the series, there are always scenes I remembered and still do. Ostagar in Origins, as well as the general feeling of struggle against overwhelming odds. In DA: Inquisition, there is the entire "The Dawn Will Come" segment that is locked into my memory forever, as well as actually feeling the spirit of the organisation you build leak into every part of the gameplay. In Veilguard? Even just a few days after finishing it, I barely remember anything that stands out. 

I eventually played through this despite having heard negative reviews on it since I am a long term fan of the Dragon Age series. I did finish it, since I wanted to be up to date on the lore and the story in case they make another one with more effort put in later on. 

And there are some nice lore tidbits included. Quite a few, actually. The issue is more with how it is presented. There are HUGE chunks of major lore or cataclysmic developments for the world presented in dialogues and letters. Meanwhile, the cast of the game is sitting in a lovely vacation lighthouse, discussing coffee and going on little bookshopping adventures.

And that is a large part of the problem. Historically these games has been very well written, with complex characters, amazing dialogue, deep lore and interesting storylines. This game... looks pretty. And draws upon what might have been pre-written lore without knowing what to do with it save for occasionally pouring it out with a bucket. 

And that makes for a very strange experience. It looks pretty, like I said. And there are a few interesting cities that I would wish to have gotten to explore more. But the characters are incredibly flat. The dialogue is very bland and one dimensional. It misses almost every opportunity to flesh out the world. 

Combat, character builds and gear choices are also very dumbed down from previous iterations of the game, which makes combat real boring and repetitive really fast. 

It does pick up a bit near the end. Once they finally abandon the vacation side quest in the world that presumably is under heavy threat but does not display even a hint of that other than in letters, the last few hours of the game is neat.

Other than that? If you are interested, then do it like me and wait to pick it up until it is on a nice discount.

[h1] [6.5/10] [/h1]
[h3] Game development seems to have started with great concepts, but clearly rushed half way through [/h3]

[i] Disclaimer: I played this game with no expectations or reading prior reviews, and this is my cumulative review. [/i]


What has stopped DA: VG from being a memorable game?

[b] 1- Bad Writing (character scripts + general storyline). [/b]
Despite a few exciting sequences, the majority of the game notably lacks nuance in story writing as much of the events feel generic or taken from a template. It follows the standard "Random protagonist gets dragged into something big and unexpected > builds a team > miraculously becomes a hero > saves the day". Events happen without proper build-up. Backstories are not explained well, where the game expects you to care about characters or events that it throws at you. Character writing is some of the worst I have ever seen. Character scripts feel like they were done by Gen AI. No depth, humanism, or any form of complexity in the sentences they utter. Every conversation is a theatre play, and the dialogue options you have are basically 3 best case scenario responses, everything is positive, no dynamism in any conversation you have. 

[b] 2- The Game treats you like a Child! [/b]
This was what really killed the experience for me. The game is extremely PG, and too frequently insults your intelligence. Your character always has these silly lines to remind you that you are the protagonist. Conversations are typically designed to dumb down information that is very easy to absorb. There is no blood or any form of gore or realism in gameplay (feels like Diablo in 3rd person). What you say means little to nothing, except in very few limited instances, where the game surprises you that in those very small instances, your choices matter. The game is steered very heavily, with little wiggle room for you to do things differently. Characters often spew lines that sound like they were taken out of a Disney movie. Excitement and unpredictability are completely absent in this game. The game is not designed for its target audience.

[b] 3- Unpolished Mechanics & Progression. [/b]
Firstly, The upgrade system stops at some point, rendering anything you collect useless after that point. Very limited ways you can improve your gear and skills (skill tree may look complex, but it's extremely basic, with limited room for creativity). Detonations are silly and feel like they were added because developers ran out of ideas. Ultimate abilities are mediocre. Game Maps are essentially platformers and limited in regards to what they offer. You will not feel the sense of 'adventure' and 'exploration' that previous DA games gave you. Finally, the game is riddled with bugs, especially with character models and maps taking too long to load. You will often run off the map or speak to floating character heads. 

[i] Overall, it is clear that the game was developed on good foundations and a lot of passion went into its concepts, art and general theme. However, the game itself did not reflect that passion as it felt like a rushed product that was prematurely released without any testing. I would be surprised if any of the developers had genuinely played the game before releasing it. [/i]

______

[b] Recommendation to Bioware: [/b] Please do not attempt a new Dragon Age game. Instead, focus on remaking Dragon Age Origins and Dragon Age 2. If the franchise is to survive after this disappointment, you will need to focus on bringing back what made it popular. Ubisoft did this, and you can follow suit.
This game is absolutely awful - railroaded button-mash crap with no roleplay potential whatsoever.

As a fan of the franchise and has just managed to get through half of Act 2 of this game:
Shout out to the writer responsible for Emmrich, he is my favorite character from all within Dragon Age series. Emmrich is the only thing that keeps me playing, and intend to finish this game. The environment in this game is gorgeous, so shout out to the team responsible for the assets as well.
 I also heard that the final mission, which was directed and crafted by the team responsible for Mass Effect, was peak, so I'll keep grinding.

This game has cheesy writing and did a poor job for most of its characters. Only Solas, Varric, Dorian, and Emmrich stayed on track with their writing standard. The fetching missions and poor puzzles almost made me quit the game too many time.
For the story, the conclusion we got for Thedas is...disappointing. The rich lore and the potential for the cameos to come back, because the world is ending YET none of the powerful factors in Thedas that is not the Veilguard group did anything. The powerful beings in Necropolis, Lich, did absolutely nothing despite their power.
The reused assets that are intended for online game are very noticable and breaks immersion. 

I hope people who work on this game reflected on this, especially the directors and heads who oversee this project. This franchise had build so much reputation from its past work. This franchise didn't deserve the woke hunting that went around the past year or two, the issue is not LGBTQ+ element in the game, it's the many issues that piled up.

I'm not LGBTQ or anything even though everyone seems to hate this title so much this is my first time playing this series and I actually enjoy the campaign and characters to interact with. I never played the first 2 games so I'm new to the series, but I like the action, gameplay and cinematic sequences are well executed here. Its graphically very demanding as you need a high end system to fully run this game , but its well optimized enough for a med to high range gpu if you have that. BioWare is known for creating some of the best RPG games that I have played before in the past. KOTOR, Mass Effect Trilogy and Anthem. Despite BioWare being owned by EA and they have a bad reputation, I hope they are able to stick around long enough to release the much anticipated Mass Effect 4 if it doesn't get cancelled somehow. Even though it seems only long time fans of the other Dragon Age games are bashing this title those new to the series like myself enjoy it so this comes down to an opinion of choice. Furthermore the game was discounted to $20 recently so for a title like this that was a substantial discount.

I'm not sure why this game gets such a bad wrap. I mean, I get it, I read people's gripes, it just not what I experienced. Coming fresh from my first playthrough of DAI, I just found this game to be much better. DAI did not age well in my opinion, so that could be part of it, but this is a really high quality game. Graphics are super nice, gameplay is solid, UI is clean, voice acting is great, music is high quality, and the story is interesting. Does it feel exactly like a Dragon Age game, no, and that's okay.

I played DA 1 and 2 a LONG time ago. I had Inquisition in my library for forever, and finally got around to it. I know some people really like that game, but I thought it was very okay. Combat was a drag and the game just goes on and on. I rarely don't do most of a game, and with DAI I couldn't wait for it to be over and skipped things I ordinarily would not. It feels like I have the reverse experience of what some people experienced with this game. I noticed people saying decisions didn't matter. I felt like they did. As far as I can tell, there are a few major decisions that impact the game, one early on, and many much later. And while the core of the game remains unchanged, the contents of the world are different. It felt meaningful to me.

While this game is more of an ARPG, I rather enjoy the combat. It's simpler in some ways, but more complex in others. I like parry and dodge being an important part of the flow of combat, if you choose. I like how intuitive detonations are in this game versus DAI. I really like the skill tree, and the fact that I can respec anytime I choose. I like feeling free to play around with different builds and not needing some hard to find resource to respec. During my playthrough, I tried a few different things and stumbled into what I think is quite a powerful build and really had fun with it.

The map is really well done. Everything is marked to let you easily track without having to create my own system of screenshots and such to track things. The level design is also really well done. The areas are all folded on top of each in a way other that makes exploring interesting and rewarding. DAI is a much older game, so I cut it some slack, but those were just giant mostly flat maps with enemies. DAI felt like barely a game, like a single player MMO. This game, feels like a story, an adventure, and also a game.

I like the gear system as well. I'd rather get a smaller amount of gear that I can improve and upgrade, instead of tons of gear that you vendor. Upgrading the gear unlocks new perks at each level. It made gear feel more meaningful and gave me something to work towards when I got a new piece of gray gear that had some good perks later on that I wanted to work towards so I could use.

Overall, I really enjoyed this game. Took me around 80 hours for one playthrough with 90% achievements. I grabbed it on sale at some point, and whatever I paid, it was worth it. Definitely recommend giving it a playthrough, whether it's your first DA game or you've played the rest.

Dragon Age Veilguard is a third person action RPG where player character Rook must assemble a team of heroes to stop a pair of Elven gods from destroying the world.

Dragon Age Origins is my second favourite game of all time (after Witcher 3), however I thought DA2 and Inquisition were disappointing in various ways. I also replayed Inquisition recently to prepare for Veilguard.

[h1]Gameplay (medium quality)[/h1]

•	Character creator is incredibly detailed, and I think I spent about an hour adjusting all the sliders to get my Rook looking just right.

•	Combat is entertaining and I enjoyed it for most of my playthrough. However, its not really what I wanted from a party based RPG. It doesn’t feel tactical at all for various reasons, and is much more action focused.

•	Playing as a mage, the combat felt like a third person shooter, and actually very similar to Mass Effect. Enemies have the same colour coded health bars – red for health, yellow for armour, blue for barrier, while spells/abilities for Rook and companions are selected from an ability wheel which pauses the action. Your companions don’t have their own health bars so they can’t get knocked out, which feels more like an action RPG.

•	Spells cost mana instead of having individual cooldowns. Most spells cost 100 mana and you start with 100 maximum mana, which meant that even though Rook can have 3 spells equipped, I could only use 1 spell at a time. Rook also has a laser beam attack and a defensive shield, which both cost mana, so using them prevents spellcasting until mana regenerates. Eventually, through the skill tree and equipped gear bonuses, I did manage to get up to 250 mana, allowing me to use spells more frequently, but it took a long time to get there.

•	Similarly, companions also have 3 abilities equipped, but using 1 puts them all on cooldown, which extremely simplifies the tactics.

•	There’s no sense of tactical positioning at all. DAO was very tactical, as I would try to use melee specialists to keep enemies away from mages/archers. Unfortunately, Bioware abandoned that style of combat starting from DA2. I felt like most of the time, every enemy in a fight would exclusively focus on attacking me, even when I ordered companions to target a specific enemy. Again, I was the party’s mage, and I couldn’t stand still for more than a couple of seconds because I’d have multiple melee enemies in my face, and multiple ranged enemies spamming projectiles at me, causing me to have to constantly dodge all over the place while wondering if my companions were doing anything at all.

•	I wouldn’t say the combat was too hard (playing on adventurer/medium difficulty), just quite stressful. I only died a few times, mostly while attempting to fight side quest bosses who were clearly labelled as a few levels higher than me, and were more easily defeated when I returned at the appropriate level.

•	Veilguard’s combat is more enjoyable than Inquisition’s though, as the enemies are much less spongy and usually die quicker.

•	Outside of combat there’s a good amount of exploring to do. At first, some levels feel like linear corridors, however a few maps that you’ll return to regularly will gradually open up into much larger areas with puzzles to solve and loot chests to find. Puzzles are often simple but fun, involving using crystals or wisps to create bridges, open doors or disable forcefields.

•	I like that collecting duplicate weapons, armour and other items upgrades them with better stats and passive bonuses. This is better than other RPG loot systems where you find loads of useless trash. However, its a shame that most loot can only be equipped on Rook, with each companion having their own unique gear. So many legendary/unique items were left gathering dust in my inventory.

[h1]Story (medium quality)[/h1]

•	Story is mostly well written and features several interesting companion characters and a couple of familiar faces from previous games. I liked my version of Rook based on my dialogue choices.

•	Main quests are often exciting and the final chapters felt like we were leading an epic scale final battle, including a Mass Effect 2 style suicide mission where your companions could die if you make the wrong choices.

•	However, the major negative in the story is that the villains just weren’t very interesting to me. I was expecting Solas to be the main villain (I think for most of its development this game was going to be called Dread Wolf), which would have been very interesting as one of our companions from Inquisition. And while I was ultimately satisfied with his ending, I think it was a mistake to sideline him for most of the game in favour of two new villains who got very little screen time to make me care about them. (Just like Corypheus was barely involved in DAI). Its not enough to just tell me that these two gods are evil (which other characters will do, repeatedly, in so many conversations), I have to actually see it to feel it. I’ll never forget how much I hated Loghain in Origins.

•	In addition to the main quest, each companion has their own multiple stage personal quest, and there are lots of side quests to help other factions and civilians.

•	There were always multiple new companion dialogues every time I returned to the team’s lighthouse base.

•	Voice acting is excellent.

•	Unfortunately the dialogue often feels just a bit too nice and jokey. You’re never allowed to actually disagree with a companion about anything.

•	During character creation you’ll select a background story for Rook. I wish they were unique playable prologues like in Origins. There were several times that other characters referred to the events of Rook’s background, but I didn’t know what to say because it all happened off screen.

•	I want my own pet griffon.

[h1]Technical (medium quality)[/h1]

•	It took me 68 hours to complete the story and all side quests.

•	Performance was mostly smooth with minimal noticeable stuttering, most settings on high and DLSS quality in 1440p.

•	Environment graphics look fantastic, as your journey will take you through city streets, forests, stone ruins, underground caves and crypts, all of which can be covered in disgusting blight growths.

•	The game only crashed one time, so its extremely stable.

•	Controls are fully rebindable and work great with mouse and keyboard.

•	There’s a graphical bug where several small dots can be seen on a character’s hair, which I noticed in many scenes throughout the game. I believe this relates to how lighting sources interact with strand hair. I didn’t want to disable strand hair because when its not bugged (for example, darker scenes), it looks really good, especially the way long hair flows naturally over the top of armour without clipping.

•	I wish I could stop companions from constantly telling me during combat that enemies are “attacking from range” every few seconds. I can see the on screen indicator of incoming projectiles. Also, stop telling me to move out of the way when an enemy uses an AOE attack, because I can see the massive red danger indicator on the floor. This kind of design feels aimed at a 5 year old who is playing their first videogame.

•	Side quests repeatedly take you back to the same locations multiple times which can feel a bit repetitive, although its not as bad as that one cave from DA2.

[h1]Recommendation[/h1]

Dragon Age Veilguard is a good game, but its not really a great RPG. Its definitely worth playing as long as you can accept what it is.

Origins is still by far the best Dragon Age game, but at least Veilguard is better than Inquisition and DA2. If Bioware ever make another Dragon Age, every member of the team should be forced to play Origins from start to finish to understand what made it great.
Terrible dialog, character models are ugly, gameplay loop is boring and they're pushing a narrative down your throat.
While it's not like the previous entries of the series, I personally don't understand the hate. I've enjoyed this and it's definitely worth picking up on sale.

Veilguard is fun. Understand that it is a Mass Effect game in combat and structure set in the Dragon Age universe, and you'll be less likely to be disappointed. I mean, you basically aren't a major software developer at this point if you don't alienate half of your fanbase by releasing an entry in your beloved RPG franchise to middling reviews because you decided that turning it into an action game would have broader appeal, right? I know that you have to justify costs and all, but I watched through the credits at 16 times speed and they still took a couple of minutes to complete, so what on earth are you even doing here? Maybe just focus on making good games that don't try to be Marvel movies and you'll be just fine.

But yeah, the story is solid and connects to a bunch of the prior games in the series, even if the antagonists are basically cartoonish. The faction and party member quests and regular interaction are all done well, even if everything ends up a bit too drawn out. Skill upgrades are interesting and the combat is fine, even if it gets repetitive. Exploration is a lot of fun and breaks up the story and combat well, even if it mostly just gets you far too much equipment that you can't feasibly use.

It's not the best BioWare game out there, but it's perfectly solid.

i played through veilguard twice on console and am now picking it up on steam after almost two years.

i've been a huge dragon age fan since origins, i waited 10 years for veilguard and i just have to say i really enjoyed the conclusion to the series!

of course theres pros and cons to the gameplay loop, writing and characters, but overall, for me, this is the perfect ending for such a long winded story. i feel like you can't really make everyone happy with such a huge fanbase and that's okay. it's definitely not a perfect game, but it has such an amazing soundtrack, visuals and emotional storytelling!

The Dragon Age games really changed over the years but i don't actually mind, since now each game is unique and has replayable features to enjoy.

10 years of waiting, and I just ended up making a DND campaign with all the ideas and themes I had hoped would be explored in this game. After all that time, the finished product hardly even feels as if it exists in the same universe as the previous games: sanitized and toothless, cowardly even. What stakes are there in saving a world that feels so artificial? Why care for characters who are not flawed living beings, but instead harmless mouthpieces that couldn't hurt a fly? What use is solid gameplay and mechanics if the game hardly justifies investing yourself further beyond a surface level?
Just play Baldurs Gate 3, or make your own DND campaign.

i'm a card carrying member of the tumblr crew, with dyed hair and pronouns and everything, and i found this game intolerable. beautiful scenery and character creation, stupid plot, stupid characters, just a waste of time and money.

I really enjoy the game, it's very different than other dragon age installments, but that's not bad just different.

other than the dialog from Taash being a little heavy handed (not because 'iTs wOkE') i literally know people like all the companions, they did a lot of cool things with the relationship system in the game.

also performance wise it has some of the best graphics for what it demands from your PC.

edit: I do see the valid critiques of the game, you cant really be an ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ or be evil adjacent.. but i also wouldn't normally see that since I  just dont enjoy those that kind of playthrough.
What a disappointment!!! After waiting so long for this it turns out to be utterly stupid. It's pretty, but that's the only good thing I can say about it.

this game is bland and feels like work. it's annoying and good lord the performance is bad. i'm actually sad. i loved origins, i tolerated da2, i liked inquisition but this? nah. i spent 60 bucks for nostalgia and it's the last time i give this corpse of a studio money.

Overall the game is fantastic. A VERY solid combat system that makes use of every button of the controller  with well designed intent. Nothing ever feels out of place, and although you have to periodically keep an eye on your cooldowns, it's never more than a fraction of a second. Nor is the GUI invasive into what you need to be paying attention to while you're fighting. It has a solid arcadey hack and slash feel to it. Nothing too deep, but developed enough to be engaging throughout the battles. The enemy health is also well balanced for the most part. (Granted how much damage you do is entirely in the hands of how the player wants to play, which is another merit)  The story is pretty solid as well. A lot of engaging side story that although aren't as syndicating in complexity, as something like Yakuza they're still entertaining enough to want to get to know more of the interactions between the characters. This is definitely a game worthy of the name BioWare (♥♥♥♥ EA though) 
I literally started another campaign immediately after I cleared it for the first time. S-tier product.

If I knew what I know now I  would not buy this game.  The load times are super long.  YOu will not be able to switch characters in game. New game creates a new character and when you try to go back to a previous character you have to use new game mode again , finalize the same character and start game.  The story stars all over again.  The save system needs some cereous repair.  It sucks   I blew away 70$ for game and add ons.  I was so stupid.

this is one of those game i think should have a mixed review setting
 
its a good action-adventure game no doubt, but...

i find its too politically correct ( for a medieval-fantasy setting)and all the characters I've come across are a little too emotionally supportive with little to no conflict.

I really enjoyed this game.  The combat was solid, story engaging and graphics excellent.  I think this one is a big win for Bioware despite the low sales.  really hope they continue this trend because this game is fantastic.

If you really enjoy story and fast paced fighting compared to the strategy the other dragon ages are i think its a good play. just wish your choices had more affects on the world around you especially 1-3 all had links to eachother.
The hate is undeserved. This is a really great game. I hope more people figure that out and give it a try.

very fun game, loved the combat, felt badass and strong, i like the story and how intense it gets sometimes. also the twist idk why i didnt realize what was happening. the best part of the game was the dialogue felt very interactive and fluid and felt responsive to the mood or vibe you give. the companion relationships develop very fun too including the romance.

my biggest complaint is the dialogue lack of options anymore. the story is quite linear now and there arent really choices. even in dialogue responses you kinda always say the same thing, just a different attitude. probably makes it easier to write the dialgoue and relationships but i miss that part of inquisition.

Listen, I have talked so much ♥♥♥♥ about this game. It's a great game, awful conclusion to the franchise. Every choice in the game in reference to the rest of the franchise seems to be designed to insult fans, mock our interest, and belittle the heart of the franchise i.e. player choices. As a stand a alone game its fun, the cast is excellent, the modeling and graphics are better than any previous game, and it balances a dreary reality with a fun vibe.

If you've never played a dragon age game this is a great place to start, if you love the series it'll drive you nuts. 

I will never understand using the last game in such a popular and profitable franchise to write such an expensive ♥♥♥♥ you to the fans, but that is exactly what Veilguard is. Kind of good if you were a fan of Solas in Inquisition but they still rewrote and ignored like 60% of his character for no reason. 

I love Rook, I love playing this game, I love the rest of the franchise, but it certainly is a divisive game. The voice actors did an amazing job, and apart from Lace Harding, I have enjoyed every playthrough.

It's pretty enjoyable IF you don't play it as a direct Dragon Age game. More like.. something set in the same world. Definitely not on the same level as the previous games. Kinda sad that you can FEEL the potential this game had and the little bits of it that remain as mere ghosts of what could have been. It's certainly pretty tho

Over all maybe 5,5/10 experience, I suspect that none of the people who made the original games work here anymore and it shows. I did finish it, but the whole story resolution was a letdown. Definitely a heavy discount purchase option.

Great music, probably the best part of the game
Encounters with Solas were some of the best scenes in the game
I dont mind the big lore expansion and changes they made
Landscapes were pretty, but character design looks super cartoony?? much worse than the previous games
There was not nearly enough time spent with companions and not alot of character development was going on there. Everyone kinda just clapped each other's backs and I didn't care for most of them whatsoever.
The whole Tash arc really disrupted any remaining immersion this game had....maybe if the writing and dialog was better it could have been digestible, but there have been much better diversity characters in the previous games who weren't so painfully annoying. If I was a person from the community, I'd be pissed off at this representation :D

I would recommend this game if you are in the mood for a fantasy RPG with some eye catching graphics and wanting to design a really cool looking character.  I am a die hard DA fan and have played all of the games but held off on this one for about a year and a bit. I was skeptical espesially after seeing some woke nonsense on the socials. Buuuuut I buckled after being bored at work and installing  the character creator and after finishing my badass looking elf I knew I had to see her in game. Luckily the game was on sale at that stage and I went for it. I am having lots of fun, if you keep your old school DA exceptations a little lower.  So in summary, do play it is you love the world, lore and characters, but get it on sale.

Si un jour World of Warcraft adopte une telle qualité artistique, je prends une retraite anticipée et je renonce à toute forme de vie sociale. Pour le reste, la découverte des lieux et l'exploration est géniale.  Et au final, ne serait-ce que pour Emmrich, Gustav et Evka, Veilguard vaut la peine d'être fait au moins une fois.

EDIT : Ca tire un peu en longeur sur la fin et c'est pas incroyablement passionnant au niveau de l'écriture toutefois. Et ces histoires de genre bien que peu présentes m'ont fait bouger une burne parce que j'en ai honnêtement rien à branler, c'est artifc'IEL.

Clearly, I'd heard a lot about this game and the general feedback/response from some elements of the community.

I don't get it. 

This game was excellent. The game is graphically gorgeous. The combat is pretty tight, if a little repetitive at times, the story arch is great and satisfying at the end(s) and it tied nicely into previous games; it felt like a genuine continuation. Some of the dialogue choices were a bit cookie cutter at times, and it falls into the Marvel trap of a lot of comebacks having to be short, pithy and witty, which isn't necessary a lot of the time, but it was otherwise a great game. 

The game is probably my second of the season, but I've loved all of them. The story was tighter and lacked much of the sidequest bloat associated with Inquisition. The areas were interesting, the puzzles were a bit simple but it all drove the story and the worldbuilding forward, and the game really shines with the worldbuilding, as most of the DA games do. 

It's a shame that the contention of some people around the non-binary character seemed to take over. It was handled pretty delicately and, far from the accusations levied against it, it wasn't shoved in your face at every opportunity. If you like the other DA games, play it. Don't let the echo chamber put you off. It feels like the next evolution of Inquisition, so if you liked that, go for it. I don't think you'll be disappointed.

Every iteration of a Dragon Age game is different. My young 13 year old heart was broken when DA 2 was released. It was not the sequel I had dreamed of and it taught me a valuable life lesson about expectations. 

Since then, I have learned to check out trailers and reviews before purchasing a game. Veilguard was no different. I knew what I was getting into. It's an action adventure game that drank the Marvel Kool-aid and added rpg elements. And with those expectations, I enjoyed my experience. Therefore, I given Veilguard a recommendation. 

However, as many people have said, the writing is inconsistent and forced, and that inconsistentancy is very noticeable. You'll be hard pressed to overlook it and it does cheapen the overall experience. 

The real crime here, in my humble opinion, is how quickly the developers/publishers abandoned this game. It's not a bad game, it just didn't land with its audience. To appease this audience, there are a number of little fixes/patches they could have implemented. As an example, dialogue options could have been edited in places to better reflect what your Rook will say. This is a minor grip, but one that could change the tone/and or create a smooth experience. Alas reader, they did not. 

Questions remain, will we see a return of Dragon Age? and if it does, will I return to Dragon Age?

*Edit* Spelling

TL:DR 
Dragon Age: Mass Effect

Don't get why there is so many negative comments. The game itself is enjoyable, the characters are quite cool but lacking any friction between them which was so great in previous game. Fighting is dynamic although I changed my character ability points many times because it gets boring fast. I wish it was more like in DA: Origins when it comes to battle and combos :(. Had a great time especially with the end game, which is rare.

My starting point to this series was Dragon Age Inquisition, I did not play 1 and 2. Therefore, the first time I played this game with an EA store subscription, I lacked a lot of context for the history of the game. 

The first time I played the game, I played it donning figurative eyewear made of extreme criticism as I expected a lot from this game, fair to say I did not like it and had to stop halfway into it. Then I bought it on steam for a discounted price and watched some history into the world since the other entries were fairly outdated.

The game offers 3 starting classes, a sword and board knight, a rogue and a mage. It is really important that you actually like the class you play, for me it got really boring playing a knight. Second time around, a rogue worked for me. There are 3 specializations within the classes too and they are quite varied, allowing you access to a decently sized skill tree.

The graphics and the artwork are significantly different from the previous entries, it appears less mature and the it fails to capture the great evils that plague this world, it feels like they aimed in the middle of something safe and then also gruesome, which looked like a little weird mix. That said, the visuals are great, the game is really smooth and I did not come across any bugs in my playthrough. Though, it failed to deliver on the ambience I expected, it is decent enough.

The character customization was excellent. Although, the basic character presets might look cartoonish without any customization, you can give your character a grim and mature look through adjusting the various sliders quite a bit.

The story lacks a bit in the early stages of the game. Character interactions seems a bit forced and your decisions will feel like they lack certain impact. However, as the game carries forward, they correct this part by introducing impactful consequences to your actions. They are not many but it is significant enough to let you know that your decision does carry weight and they will weigh on you. It doesn't do a great job of tying past game content or the history of the world, it could have been better but I still liked the amount of history that it does introduce and the amount of stuff you can read on the codex. If you stick with it, it gets better in the last half of the game and it becomes a great ride with your companions.

Most of the companions in this game is quite new to you and there is no prior history of them. Therefore, it gets a bit difficult to relate with them, feel compassion for them or even deal with them. The only familiar face is Harding and it was only because I played DAI.

The best part of the game was the combat and how smooth it was. Throughout the game, it didn't feel repetitive or feel frustrating (if you play on the hardest difficulty enemies do feel like sponges especially if you are under leveled for fights). You can make builds around different items you come across and combat is kept fresh that way.

TLDR: Not a GOTY contender but definitely recommended to get on sale!
It's not what I want from DA, ofc, but it's not a big game. Mediocre DA game, but ok'ish - solid game overall.

As a fan of Dragon Age: Origins, I really enjoyed Dragon Age: The Veilguard.

1. The story was excellent and kept me engaged throughout the game.

2. The companions were interesting, but the interactions with them didn't fully work for me. The game doesn't really allow the player to be harsh or confrontational. If I want to tell someone off, the game should let me do that instead of constantly softening the dialogue. Dragon Age: Origins is a great example of this, where you could argue with companions and even dislike them, which made the world and characters feel more alive. I would also remove the dialogue hint icons and let players interpret their choices on their own.

3. I liked the level design, but I wasn't a fan of the overall visual style. The game looked too bright, friendly, and somewhat cartoonish. Dragon Age is a dark fantasy setting, and I think the visuals should reflect that more consistently.

4. I really enjoyed the combat, especially playing as a mage. However, I would redesign the progression system by focusing more on active abilities and fewer passive bonuses. I would also increase the number of abilities available during combat. Instead of being limited to only a few skills, I would prefer having access to more abilities through hotkeys, similar to older RPGs.

5. Solas was one of the highlights of the game for me. Learning about his past, his motivations, his struggles, and the consequences of his actions was genuinely fascinating.

6. The game's performance was excellent. I experienced no noticeable technical issues or slowdowns while playing on an NVIDIA RTX 3060.

I honestly don't understand all the hate this game receives. In my opinion, it's a very good game and far more enjoyable than Dragon Age II and Inquisition. If the developers had embraced a darker visual style, it would have been even better.

That said, when compared to Dragon Age: Origins, most of the companions in Veilguard fall short in terms of depth, complexity, and overall memorability.

I would definitely recommend the game. I spent 52 hours with it and don't regret a single one. If Dragon Age 5 ever happens, I hope it embraces a darker tone in its visuals, dialogue, and player choices.

I have hundreds of hours in this game on my Xbox, playing it on PC and having it so much closer and with so much more detail/quality is amazing. I absolutely adore this game, and I plan to put time into playing it on here too. Absolutely worth buying twice.

So, this game is Great for what it is. 

It's pretty well known that Bioware and the Dragonage team really struggled witht he production of this game. Mainly because of the awful Trend amoung companies to cling to the Live Service Game fad. Which 1000% awful from the go. 

In spite of being told Several times to change the game frame work, to Live Service to Single Player RPG, To Live Service again and some how settling between the 2.  I will admit that this game is a Ginormous failure, It undersold and is not what fans were expecting. 

Here's where the game excel's. 
Game play is fluid, the Story is coheesive and enjoyable it's a otherwise Good AAA Fantasy Action RPG with I felt Solid Representation

Here's where it fails
Your choices from previous games were discarded. this was a cornerstone of why DragonAge games did well, the world felt living and breathing and as a result infintley more engaging. The lingering liveservice elements stink,and it lacks replayability unless you are obssessed like yours truly.

That said in the end I really enjoyed it.

A solid entry in the Dragon Age canon. Great characters and a fun story. I felt like this one escaped a lot of the bloat of DA Inquisition, felt more finished than DA 2 and is obviously much more modern than DA Origins. Despite the world hopping, the game managed to make me feel attached to some of the locations while keeping the world big and diverse. As ever with DA, there were feels in spades.

This is the most generic you can possibly get as an RPG game. If it wasn't a Dragon age game i would say its fine for an a company making its first RPG.... but it isn't... its bloody Dragon age. How could you in the right mind release a dragon age game in this state? its utter dog S__T.

Pros: 
mean it works i guess...
if you never played any other Dragon age game or are somewhat challenged at simple things, then its fine as a game.
combat is ok, not great but not terrible

Cons:
They destroyed existing characters by literally tossing out their personalities and replacing them with new ones. Character Arch? No, here's a bloody cliff & no explanation as to why they changed, I guess you can call it a case of stolen identity. 

want weapon variety like any of the previous game? No! you don't even get great swords. Granted, they said they were reducing the variety to "stream line the process & make the existing weapons feel better"..... but my god if this is the extent of what they meant by "better"..... they need to Fire who ever he/she/they/them/fat/whateverIdontcare is, they practically destroyed this IP.

Story = Bad this game has made me so exhausted from frustration i don't even want to bother.

Conclusion:

if your an original fan don't buy this game, its a massive insult to the existing IP and a slap in the face to all previous fans, however if your like me and still hoping its not as bad as the original reviews stated... wait for a huge sale if you cannot resist temptation. (I couldn't either, and didn't believe a company like Bioware would do something like this to such a beloved IP; but i was wrong.)

if your new to the franchise, buy some other game, there's a lot of better games out there in the genre than this....

Veilguard has a great story, some pretty good characters, and easily the best combat and visuals in the series. It's Mass Effect in the Dragon Age universe. And yes, that's a bit more action-focused than the first two entries in the series, but I never really felt like those games nailed their more tactical combat anyway so I'm VERY pleased that they overhauled it and just focused on excitement.

I can legitimately understand someone being upset that the combat style was changed, even if I was very happy with the change. But I'm pretty sure that most of the negative reviews for this game are because there's a nonbinary squadmate and there's like ten total minutes of story about that that does indeed feel kind of hamfisted. It is my sincere opinion that most of the people writing negative reviews of this game need therapy, friends, or both.

It's not terrible by any means. But if someone asked me if they should play it, I would likely tell them to skip it. My favourite Dragon Age game is Dragon Age Origins. It's impossible for a series are long as varied as Dragon Age to be able to please all their fans from every entry. If your favourite game is Dragon Age 2 then you may in fact love this game. But, for me, it's too shallow and formulatic. 
The best thing, and what clearly got all the attention, is the characters. The party members all have pretty interesting quests and are really well developed. Even just running into them in your hub is always enjoyable as there's so much dialogue. The way they interact with each other, even dating if you don't pick someone for yourself, is compelling.
The graphics are also very pretty, I don't mind the new art style, but it is symptomatic of the fact that this is no longer dark fantasy of the first game, but is solidly high fantasy, which I don't like as much. There are no elven alienages, no addiction to lyrium, and all the factions you ally with are just too good. The Crows, for example, should be way more evil, the way Zevran had talked about them. Everything just seems like the edges were sanded off. It's a "nice" experience with one dimensional villians and everyone else is good and likeable. I like more grit in my characters.
The combat can also become a chore. Almost every encounter has shielded mages in the back and melee people in the foreground and ranged attackers off to the sides. Over and over again. There's so many dragons and they all fight exactly the same way with only an elemental difference.
There's treasure all over the place, and I like that your items level up rather than just being added to a growing junk pile. But, there's no real way to focus on a weapon you really want. If you find one you want to level up you're just out of luck, you have randomly find it in the world or in a shop. The crafting component based equipment level up system at the base is also just busywork and annoying.
I think a big problem here is that I just finished Clair Obscur and this game is just so basic in compairison. It's not doing anything really new or interesting. It's just sort of a nice adventure. And that's not enough for me to recommend it.
The first game was awesome and made contributions to the genre. 2 was pretty bad. Inquisition was really cool. every thing after that has been complete trash.

I held off buying this game based on the mixed reviews.  The storyline is actually really good.  The combat isn't perfect -- it's a little messy, but still fun.    Honestly the only thing that bugs me sometimes is the art style, its sort of "childish" for lack of a better word.  Overall fun game I would reccommend

Just about every negative thing I have read about this game prior to buying it revolved around the fact that you * CAN * play as a gay, trans or non-binary character. Note that * CAN * does NOT equal * MUST * and it is very easy to not interact with those aspects of the game at all. If you don't want to play that kind of character, then simply don't. The options are there for the people that want them and can be ignored/skipped by the people that don't.
Different than the previous iterations, but a good game nonetheless.

Great game with amazing gameplay, a compelling story and fun companions. I don't know how it holds up to the rest of the series since I only played inquisition and Veilguard, but I'm loving it.
I especially love the new inclusion of trans and nonbinary experience and dialogues specific to these. 
I also really like that your identity and background overall (specie and faction) have an impact in the dialogues.

Overall, an amazing game that any Dragon Age fan should play.

The positives:
- The graphics were top notch!
- The level design was beautiful and extensive.
- The sound effects were perfect and the music very good.
- The gameplay (at least the warrior side - didn't play the other classes) was both fun and challenging.
- The main storyline writing was engaging.

The negatives:
- The companion storylines felt cheesy and predictable (save Taash's.  Theirs connected even if controversial to some).  Also, not a fan of Manfred; that character was weird.
- The dialogue between companions often felt disjointed during conversations at time, making them unenjoyable.  I know this is difficult when you have branched dialogues, but perhaps the scaling back the amount of dialogue choices would make them more impactful and engaging.
- The armors...  While unique in a lot of regards, it felt odd that a game with a rating of Mature and upper body nudity seemed to hide the human body when it came to the armor.  Not saying they need to all be risque, but it felt like the characters were either wearing several layers of carpet or 200lbs of metal.
- The main character body selection was very limited and the textures for the main character looked off.

But once again, this is an amazing game that the developers should be very proud of.  Even despite lackluster sales, I really hope they do make another and focus on writing, writing, writing!!!

This is a great capstone for the Dragon Age series, and brings the ending of Inquisition to a satisfying finale.

The gameplay is stellar: you can tune the difficulty up to higher settings if you like action RPGs, because it can get pretty intense and feel very rewarding when you go through entire fights without getting hit, setting up combos, and making the most of directing your teammates and optimizing your build and gear.  Or you can tune the difficulty down and breeze through the game for the story.

The character customization is also top-notch, though you can't switch classes—you're locked in with the class you choose at the start. (You can immediately customize appearance and gear for stats separately, and the game features excellent fantasy fashion. A huge win for us Fashion Souls players.) Being stuck with a single class is not to the game's detriment, though. There's a learning curve to understanding and exploiting game mechanics like combos, teamwork, stagger bars, synergies, and stacking buffs/debuffs. You can, for example, play high-risk-high-reward builds, or play it safe. You can min-max, or try to be more balanced in your tactics and builds. The game gives you a lot of options. You can always reset your skill points and experiment with different builds, and the game offers a lot of variety and interesting specializations for all three classes. And it's just very fun to figure out a setup that works, and best enemies you're underleveled for!

I also appreciate how this game respects your time as a player. The achievements are not locked behind making you re-play it a dozen times to see multiple romances or outcomes, it encourages you to stick with your choices, good and bad, and see the story through. Many of your decisions come into fruition very late in the game, but they offer satisfying payoffs, and don't pus them into hypothetical sequels—something that has often bothered me in previous Bioware RPGs. This game is great for role-playing purists.

People saying the writing in this game is bad are dead wrong. The line-by-line dialogue is great, with some real bangers dropped, especially in the second half of the game. That even goes for ambient dialogue and Codex notes you can read in between the action, if you're interested. It's worth finding 100% of the Codex, even if there's no achievement attached, because it adds so much to the world and characters. The characters are charming, their story arcs are deeply intertwined and compelling, and the pacing of the game is outstanding, where it juggles the action and exploration with its quest beats effortlessly. The villains are a bit cartoonish, but ultimately understandable if you read between the lines and explore deeply enough, and offer up spectacular and satisfying boss fights in turn. And they feel like a fitting escalation from the villains of previous games.

Don't listen to the haters on this one. If you enjoyed all three previous Dragon Age games, you owe it to yourself to play this one through to the ending at least once.

Play Origins instead
 The gameplay and aesthetic customization character creation are great in this game.. the story, worldbuildling, lore and everything else.. mid

Having only joined the Dragon Age Fandom with Inquisition, 2 years after that game's release, at that, I don't really understand the hate against Veilguard. I will admit that in terms of the story development, Veilguard is a slow burn/slow start, but the storytelling really picks up after Weisshaupt. One thing I absolutely support BioWare having done is drastically reducing the side mission onslaught that you get to experience with Inquisition, with the side missions in Veilguard feeling much more meaningful and not just a hamfisted attempt to add in arbitrary and meaningless missions to artificially increase your playtime. The graphics are amazingly stunning and I appreciated the action RPG elements more than most. My least favorite part of Veilguard is arguably the UI, but I am biased since I absolutely loved Inquisition so your mileage may vary in that aspect. 

Generally speaking, I don't consider the argument using "wokeness" as the reason to label any game bad or dismiss it as a valid argument. Especially when it comes to BioWare games. The same arguments being used in 2026 to criticize Veilguard in many a reddit comment section were the same ones being used 10+ years ago regarding Dorian, only for them to pull a complete 180 on those opinions in 2024. That being said, Taash arguably has the weakest character writing of any of the companions, and I can understand the hate surrounding discussions of Taash. It feels as though the writing surrounding Taash was done by committee and the result was a very infantilized character that is very easy to dislike given the rather superficially written personality that is attempting to be politically correct while simultaneously being the most abrasive character and failing at both. 

The locations you will go to are amazing and feel appropriate given that the 1st three games took place in Southern Thedas. Treviso was my favorite place in the game. The twilight atmosphere happening in the city were the sun has just set and the streets are dark but the sky is still partially sunlit is beautiful. 

I would argue that Veilguard is not really a spiritual sequel or successor to any of the 3 previous games but is more of a spin-off of the series whose events happen adjacent to the events of Inquisition. If you pick up this game as a first-time enjoyer of the series and go into the game with that mindset, you will have a much better time than everyone else who is going into this with the preconceived notions of what Dragon Age is supposed to be.

A worthy conclusion to the Dragon Age franchise. It answers many questions that were raised in Dragon Age Origins already, and in the rest of the games. It truly felt like a sequel to Inquisition especially.
 
Despite the fact that EA made developing this game a really hard task (as can be read in many articles), I do think they managed to create an actual good game. It’s not perfect, but it’s very enjoyable. I was waiting for it to become bad, and it never did. Many other players’ reviews made it seem as if the game was trash. However, this turned out to be a massive exaggeration. Having replayed Inquisition right before playing the Veilguard, the game did improve on many of its mechanics and most of its issues. It felt like they did the franchise justice, even though development was very rocky. 

The story was satisfying, and the progression of it made sense and felt natural. There were quite a few interesting twists and turns. The antagonists had reasonable motivations that fit their characters, and they felt like true threats to the world of Dragon Age. There were several choices throughout the narrative that you could make, with one choice having the most major repercussions. There were also some other minor choices that affected moments in small ways. Considering the game’s development, I was happy with the choices that were offered. The choices you make impact the endings that you can achieve. I got the ending that is considered to be the “best”, and this felt like a gratifying conclusion to me.

The companions were well-written and compelling characters, and the romances were comparable in content to Inquisition and the other Dragon Age games. I do think that it was slightly disappointing that all the companions always get along and nobody has any friction with each other. The same is true for the playable character Rook. It is pretty much impossible to be mean to the companions. I do assume that most of this is due to the game’s development history, and while it does seem like you’re a therapist sometimes, I still enjoyed the companion dynamics. And it was a nice surprise that the companions that you don't romance can actually romance each other.

The environments and graphics looked absolutely gorgeous. There were so many unique and eye-catching locations that it never felt like I was in a repetitive area. The combat was pretty fun and way better than in Inquisition. It was more engaging and interactive, plus the enemies weren’t boring bullet-sponges. It did get a bit repetitive near the end, but this was an issue in all the other Dragon Age games as well. As for the audio, the sound design was absolutely solid and always delivered in every key moment. The music was always fitting. The voice acting was very good as well, and touching. The voice actors’ performances were really emotional.

All in all, this is a Dragon Age game that is definitely worth playing if you played the previous installments. It lives up to what the other games established. I had a really fun time with the Veilguard and wouldn’t hesitate to recommend it.

I get the hate behind the game. There is really an agenda in this game that loses some of that OG charm of Dragon Age. Taash has to be one of the worst characters ever written. With that being said the game is still great.  I found the combat to be the best out of all the games. There is still a lot of enjoyable characters that I grew very fond of and didnt want to lose. Your choices matter, more so than any other game in the series. I picked the game up for 20 bucks and dont regret it. Seems to be a lot of hate by people who were just really into the first game and they hate every dragon age after it, so take it with a grain of salt. If you liked the others its a solid game.

I came into this game knowing how much everyone seemed to hate it, so my expectations were pretty low. It's definitely not as expansive and choice-driven as the previous games. The choices you do get seem to get pretty streamlined, and one or two of them have pretty atrocious consequences that you really cannot avoid one way or another. The game seems to hold your hand for the entire run, where you pretty much know at all times what missions are available and where, which is great, really, but it also shows how small the world actually is, because you roam the areas pretty quickly in order to complete said missions, even without fast travel. People complained about how dialogue choices barely deviate from one to the other, and to be fair, yeah you can tell that sometimes the options almost remind you of Persona 5 options, where you read both options aloud and you could perfectly fit the two together in one sentence.

With all of that being said, I also have to appreciate the gameplay, which, despite the growth tree being overly simplistic in its nature, was actually quite fast-paced and easy to fall into. Even if I took a few months on a break from the game, everything came back to me at the speed of light. The battles are visual as heck, and even though sometimes the enemies would just... not give you a single bit of room to actually perform any attacks, you get just enough time to work around it so you can actually beat them. Even though the dialogues suffer, it was quite fun seeing the story develop, especially because since you went back to the same areas all the time you also got to interact with secondary characters who aren't part of your team a lot more. I found that refreshing considering in previous DA games you usually visit an area, complete it and move on to the next. Usually. It showed a bit more familiarity with these other people, to the point where I kind of grew just as attached to them as I would to my team of ragtag misfits/crazy powerful experts. The lore also expands greatly from where we were at back with the DA:I DLCs, building up a few important new pieces of history that hadn't been explored, and I find it quite interesting to hear and read about. As for the main plot, some bits I hated, but some other bits I loved, because somehow they reminded me a bit of the previous entries. Of how great and immersive this world is.

Anyway, TL;DR, game has lots of faults, but if you come in knowing that, you can still have lots of fun.
This is a classic example of bad new games, better graphics, better looks and thats it, plus i saw pronouns at the character creation, 4 me, insta FAIL!

Dragon Age: The Veilguard — A Masterpiece That Trips Over Itself
------------------------------------
Probably the best game in the series mechanically. Possibly the most frustrating to recommend.

Combat
------------------------------------
The best Dragon Age has ever felt. Small and large-scale encounters both work, and the action is fluid without being brainless. Some classes need rebalancing, but for a single-player game that's a minor footnote, not a dealbreaker.

RPG and Story
------------------------------------
Your decisions carry weight — genuinely Game of Thrones-level weight in places. You can't play a villain, but that's true of most BioWare titles and rarely a problem. The companion system is strong: each character has a real backstory, personality, and arc. Voice acting is excellent across the board. The story has some genuinely surprising moments, and while map reuse for story missions gets tedious, the world is large enough that it doesn't become suffocating.

Where It Falls Apart: Immersion
------------------------------------
The biggest bug in this game isn't performance or writing. It's immersion — and it's self-inflicted.
The Marvel-style banter is a matter of taste. Fine. What isn't fine is the relentless didacticism around LGBT representation. I don't object to LGBT characters — BG3 had plenty and handled it seamlessly because it was woven into the world rather than announced. Veilguard lectures. There's a difference between representation and curriculum, and this game consistently crosses that line.
Then there's character design. BioWare built its reputation partly on companions you actually got attached to — and for a large part of its audience, that attachment had a visual component. Morrigan. Isabela. Characters with presence, personality, and yes, appeal. That tradition is gone. Veilguard's female cast is visually flattened — deliberately so. There's no Eve, no Tifa, no Aerith, no 2B, no Claire, no Jill. Not even echoes of what BioWare itself once did. The male companions trend uniformly androgynous. When the primary audience — largely male — feels nothing toward the romance targets, the entire companion investment system quietly dies. You complete their quests. You don't care. That's a catastrophic failure for this genre.
The cultural design consistency breaks things further. Western fantasy settings have an established aesthetic language. Veilguard drops East Asian cultural aesthetics onto elves with zero narrative grounding — no lore reason, no geographic logic. I'm Chinese. I enjoy seeing East Asian culture in games when it makes sense. Give me a Wuxia world, an eastern civilization where Qi powers combat, and I'm invested. Slapping those aesthetics onto elves in a Western fantasy isn't representation, it's incoherence. You'd flag the same problem if Viking dwarves appeared in a Wuxia epic and were called inclusive.

The result is a game that keeps ejecting you from its world — not through bugs or bad writing, but through design decisions that prioritise agenda over vision.

Verdict
------------------------------------
Mechanically excellent. Narratively decent. Immersively broken by choices that didn't need to be made this way.
BioWare built this franchise on a simple formula: great combat, meaningful choices, and companions you genuinely cared about. Veilguard delivers on the first two. It abandons the third. When you can't get attached to the characters you're supposed to romance, when the world's visual logic doesn't hold together, and when the game stops trusting you to accept representation without being taught it — the emotional core hollows out. What should feel like an epic RPG starts feeling like a very polished arcade game with cutscenes.
The tragedy is that the bones are brilliant. The combat alone deserves a better game around it. But authentic representation and world-building coherence are not in conflict — developers just have to choose craft over checklist. Veilguard chose the checklist.

Recommended with reservations. Play it for the combat and the story beats. Just don't expect to fall in love with anyone along the way — and in a BioWare game, that's the most damning thing you can say.

One of the few games that didn’t irritate me at all: the interface is intuitive and comfortable to use, I encountered no bugs, and the game runs very smoothly.

The story felt confusing at first, and the companies seemed a bit generic, but it improved over time. Overall, I really enjoyed playing the game.

dragon age is the franchise that got me into gaming, and it kind of devastates me that this is going to end up being the last one. it's a gorgeous game with some of the best combat in the series, and there are so many areas of the game that are just powerhouse areas--like the seige on weisshaupt--that should go down as some of the best set pieces in the series. the character creator is mind-blowing and some of the choices you can make to build up your character--including being able to toy with your character's gender and sexuality in ways that effect dialogue--are super cool. i loved seeing characters pop up from the dragon age books, comics, and shows (especially my little grey wardens), and i love the relationships you can build with your companions and the ways your investment in them can effect outcomes later in the game.

the tragedy of this game is all of the meddling behind the scenes that led to the writers not having enough time to flesh out the things that they wanted to. swapping from a multiplayer game to single player and having to rebuild so much of the story in a short turnaround was a knife to the throat here. there are things that come off as clunky that you can tell they just did not have enough time to expand on in the way that they wanted to--especially with characters like taash. beautiful, important concepts that just did not get the space to breathe that they needed. 

this game also unfortunately nukes the lore of the rest of the franchise in a way that has soured my ability to replay the older games. what is the point of struggling with saving thedas if you know that when it comes down to it, by the end of the fourth game, it will be gone no matter what you do?

i'm still putting this as a recommendation because i think that if you're playing this as just a game on its own, it is a deeply entertaining and fun experience. but as a franchise installment (and ultimately a series ender) it is a pretty bummer note to end on.

It's not perfect by a long shot, but it turned out remarkably well despite EA's meddling. Yes, it's different from the first three (which also had their differences). It's ok to let them be different. Great visual and sound design, overall entertaining story, fun and wonderfully voiced characters that I got quite attached to (with one twist that really took me by surprise), and a satisfying ending. I did miss the old RTWP combat system a bit, but still enjoyed the action-oriented system adapted from Mass Effect.

I really enjoyed this game. The story and side missions are great too!

Story: 9/10
Visuals: 8/10
Gameplay: 9/10

Crazy fun game. A good narrative driven game where the dialogue gets a bit goofy at times and it is slow to start, but once you get into it you can run around for hours making fun in Thedas. I would play it again

not every game is for everyone and this game certainly has some things that I hate (Taash, obvious ideology pandering)  But overall this game is super fun, combat is fun and the story and characters aren't terrible.  

Overall a gorgeous romp through thedas :D
amazing game, ridiculously over-hated for so many laughable reasons, we live in 2026 guys not in medieval times
The writing is so bad, its cringe. The actors are way too emotional and weak and the trans stuff really does take away from the game (I thought I wouldn't mind it, but it's WAyyy too much)
Everyone has different expectations but for me this one definitely met them! I will say the romance wasn't the best though. The story was great and I loved how my decisions mattered.
Stopped working. Runs at like 5fps. Stupid.

I love the Dragon Age Franchise and wish they would make more games in the series, I love that I can have romance options and that I can even settle down with whomever I choose. I enjoy every minute of my time in the world of Dragon Age.

Beautiful visuals, an engaging combat system with party combos and interdependent builds, and a party and faction system that takes you across the breadth of Thedas' worldbuilding. The narrative did a great job of brining together storylines that have been building across the Dragon Age franchise for years, making a great coda for the series.

It was fun!  Gameplay was good, story was solid, lots of quests, etc., drove me thru to the end.  Couple of minor things:

* If you do all the quests, beef up your sidekicks, max your allies, etc., you'll be OPd for the end-game, which was fine - payoff for the time commitment, I suppose.
* Lot of talking/cutscenes.  A LOT.  Have snacks/drinks handy.
* Sidekicks were fine, but as others have mentioned, a little flat.
* Choices didn't feel as impactful as they might have been.

That all said, hey, I still had fun, absolutely solid bang for your buck, and if you loved the last games, you'll dig this.
Just felt like such a let down after Inquisition. Did NOT live up to the hype.

LGBTQ nonsense if forced into the storyline of a franchise that used to be fighting off the blight and navigating a world  where you try and save the realm overshadowed by progressive ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. I can understand wanting to be represented however choose your BS during character creation at most not forcing a pronoun selection or agenda in the storyline. Ruined this franchise for me i've played through all the other title several times and i cant get through this one once.

For what Bioware had to go through to push to release this game? I'd say it was a good attempt.
Is it the best game ever made? No, not really. Is it even the best dragon age game? Seriously doubt anything can live up to what people think the BEST dragon age game is.
I'm a "new fan" (I played through all the games, somehow without spoilers, from origins to now veilguard for the past year or so) so I can't say anything about "waiting for 10 years only to be let down". This game isn't nearly as ♥♥♥♥ as people are making it out to be. I liked the expansion of many areas of the lore, some call backs and different directions with some characters - it's not a perfect game, so of course some of the writing isn't exactly top tier. Despite some of it's more safe writing and the occasional corny line, I've enjoyed the game so far! EA should've just let the writers cook from the start instead of pushing their live service ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.
Love the visuals, art direction really cooked here, I can't say anything bad about the music either - the scoring is stunning. For what it is, I love the franchise, and even if this isn't "the best game", I still like it.

It's not perfect by any means and it left a lot more loose ends from previous games than I would have liked. But it's fun for what it is and scratched a lot of my lore itches. Don't go in expecting it to be at the same level as previous games and you're more likely to have a good time.

Despite the criticism I did have fun with this game, though the fetch quests and story telling can be frustrating. If you enjoy this kind of game, get it on sale. Worth a try, but probably not something for those looking for a game that feels like Dragon Age.
It wasn't my favorite of the Dragon Age games, but I still really enjoyed this game, for the most part. The graphics and scenery in the game were beautiful.

Only played it a little bit but the game has some memory leak. Runs fine for the first 30 min and then perfomance dips. After restart it is fine again. I doubt this is only an issue with 6 gb VRAM cards like mine.

Found some workaround trough the MODloader thing but i think this is a massive issue for most casual gamers that want to try this out.
Probably the worst game i have played in 10 years or so, gameplay is dry but the writing and characters are what make this a horrendous gaming experience.

I tried. I really did.

But this game has no soul like the previous Dragon Age titles had. I acknowledge that there were things about 2 and Inquisition that didn't jive for people due to gameplay purposes or reused assets (moreso in 2 due to Bioware's financial state at the time before/during the whole EA merger thing), but you can't deny the lore and writing and acting from the characters was still there and as alive as it was in the first title when we all first encountered this series and it's gorgeous world and lore. To this day I am still a Dragon Age fan because I refuse to let what this steaming pile did to the lore and morality of the franchise ruin what were several runs and hundreds if not thousands of hours spent thoroughly enjoying the kind of stuff Bioware used to make.

The problem is Veilguard wasn't made by those same folks, and it shows like a giant neon sign on a city street in the dead of night. You can't ignore it, it's obnoxious and loud both audibly and visually, and you just want to get away from it as fast as possible. So much of the dialogue is excessively cringy sarcasm from people that don't sound like they've ever acted much in their lives, and a lack of actual stakes and shades of grey like choices and decisions in the games used to be.

The fact they also go out of their way to have you gte in contact with just about zero of the older companions or npcs we've had personal dealings with also tells a lot - I'm not saying it needs to be nostalgia city with old faces showing up every 2 minutes, but when basically NONE do despite their being dozens of major faces from the past 3 titles, and any that do feel forced or gutted from their previous iterations really doesn't do the game any favours either.

I still get the Dragon Age itch from time to time, but the only thing that'll satisfy that is either the occasional fanfiction or reinstalling an older title and playing it again for a bit. This game didn't and never will scratch that itch unless they were to somehow go back in time and keep a lot of...whatever this mess was as far from the drawing board as is humanly possible.

Overall, it's a solid game—a 7.5/10 for me. 102.7 hours played to complete my first playthrough. 

Maybe my expectations were really low for this game since I'm just playing it now. I know the valid critiques about the mechanics of the game, the story, and a lot of bigotry from people regarding Tash being NB. Despite all this, as a fan of the Dragon Age franchise with Origins being my favorite by far there was no way public opinion would make me not play this game.

So, to keep this short, if you really love the Dragon Age games, this one is going to be disappointing on the mechanics. However, this has been the case since DA2. Inquisition was an improvement and gave us hope it would get better, but here we are. For an RPG gamer like myself, this is not the type or style of gameplay I enjoy the most.

With that said, it's still a video game and a solid RPG. The battles can get repetitive, but it did not feel boring to me since the game does a good job pacing missions that are just conversations with missions that have more combat.
