Also, the missions or levels are not very long, so it's not tedious. There is no grinding for levels in this game, so that helps a lot, and your level progresses alongside the missions you complete.

I played on Underdog difficulty because I like to exhaust most of the content for all the games I play, and that can cause you to become overleveled and too OP. Early and mid-game was a challenge, especially because I was playing as a mage, enemy aggression is very high, and they always gun for your character if you are in range. Luckily, I specialized as a control mage, and by the late game, I was just freezing enemies to death.

The story was really good in my opinion. That is something games that are not open-world are better at, since you do not get sidetracked exploring as often. Despite this, the cities are big, especially Treviso, Arlathan, and Minrathous. There were not a lot of side quests as well, which is good for a game like this, and most side quests are related to the main story. The Elven gods and Solas were compelling villains, and they get introduced to you early on in the game, along with the destruction they can cause.

The companions were a good surprise. At the beginning, I was not attached, but by the end of it, Bellara, Neve, Emmrich, and Harding were among my favorites, which I didn't expect. Especially the sisterhood between Bellara and Neve—that was beautiful to experience and felt natural since I used both together to form a group of three control mages that enemies were not able to touch by the late game.

I used them all, though, each time I was in their cities and doing missions with their respective factions. This way, you get more dialogue. I made sure to mix up the pairings to see how they would interact during missions, and took the time to visit the Lighthouse often to hear their conversations.

The final mission was really good. This is something this game did really well, and honestly, it's the last impression you get, so I guess the good reviews are from people who actually finished the game.

I hope Dragon Age does not die after this, not selling well. The criticism is valid, and I agree this does not feel like an authentic Dragon Age game, but for me, neither was DA2, and we got Inquisition which was better (not better than Origins, though).

At this point, I do not expect huge changes, but I hope the recent success of classical RPG games with a twist like BG3 and Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 shows the suits that real-time fighting, diluted skill management, and simplified equipment management may not be a recipe for success nowadays. Casual gamers are not going to play the next Dragon Age; the real fans who have loved this world since Origins are the ones who can make this franchise a success again.

So, here's to hoping, since I feel where the story ended here leaves them with a lot of opportunities to get creative.

This has been one of the most fun single player experences I have played through in a bit. This game was recommended to me over their representation of trans/ nonbinary storylines. But this game is more than the "woke" representation. The story is great and each charcater is well written with lovely personalities.
'Veilguard' is easy to pick up, even for someone previously unfamiliar with the series, is full of endearing characters, has an intriguing plot, and has very satisfying combat mechanics.
I enjoyed every minute of Veilguard and each of my companions' side quests. Beautiful game world - I just wish it included a time cycle and dynamic weather.

Great lore (the Elves & Elven gods), voice acting, visuals and the characters. Combat is super fun and action-like. 4 classes each with 3 specializations, and most are good. Choices matter and the results are noticeable. Often what you say or do is brought up later on in dialogue and I like that a lot. There are a ton of cutscenes and if you like how your character looks, its' really nice

I went in with low expectations based on the mixed reviews Id heard since release. I was pleased by the gameplay, combat, ability to constantly change skills anywhere outside of combat, character designs, VO work, design of locations we had yet to visit, and the ability to change your entire character gender/design (not background or class) whenever you please using the mirror, and overall narrative. My only gripes boil down to 1) a clunky set of cutscenes that couldve easily been one instead of five 2) a lack of Dagna,
Inquisition is a masterpiece compared to this gnarly pile of dog water

Dragon Age: The Veilguard isn't Dragon Age: Origins.

And once I stopped expecting it to be, I had a great time.

The combat is faster, more action-focused, and far more cinematic than previous entries. Building your character, combining abilities with companions, and taking on larger encounters feels satisfying throughout the adventure. While longtime fans may miss some of the older tactical systems, the combat remains engaging and fun in its own right.

The real highlight is the companions. As always, BioWare excels at creating characters who feel distinct, memorable, and worth getting to know. Their personal stories, banter, and interactions often became just as important to me as the main quest itself.

Visually, the game is beautiful. The environments are packed with detail, the art direction is strong, and many locations feel like they were pulled directly from a fantasy painting. Exploring Thedas has never looked better.

The story has its ups and downs, but it successfully delivers the sense that you're part of a major conflict with world-changing stakes. There are plenty of memorable moments, interesting lore revelations, and character-driven scenes that kept me invested from start to finish.

Will every Dragon Age fan love every change? Probably not.

But judged on what it is rather than what some people wanted it to be, The Veilguard is a polished, enjoyable fantasy RPG with strong characters, excellent presentation, and a lot of heart.

**Pros:**

* Memorable companions
* Fun action combat
* Beautiful environments
* Strong character interactions
* Great world-building

**Cons:**

* Less tactical than earlier Dragon Age games
* Some pacing issues
* Not every change will appeal to longtime fans

**Overall: 8.5/10**

The reason I stayed wasn't the combat.

It wasn't the graphics.

It was because I genuinely wanted to see what happened to the people traveling beside me.

And that's always been Dragon Age's greatest strength.
if you love hair animation and hate coherent story, you will love this game!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
